2004-10-01.title: Blair to Enter Hospital to Correct Heart Problem
2004-10-01.abstract: British Prime Min Tony Blair, speaking to BBC, announces that he will undergo surgery to repair irregular heartbeat; clarifies intention to serve out full four-year term if re-elected, which would make him longest serving Labor prime min; surpassing Margaret Thatcher, Conservative icon; some politicians have speculated Blair would hand over post to political partner Gordon Brown, who is currently Chancellor of the Exchequer, partway through term (M)

2004-10-01.title: U.S. Aide Faults Serbia for Not Handing Over War Crimes Suspect
2004-10-01.abstract: Marc Grossman, United States under secretary of state for political affairs, expresses growing disappointment in lack of progress made by Serbian Pres Boris Tadic's reformist government to arrest and hand over former Bosnian Serb commander Gen Ratko Mladic to Hague tribunal; Mladic is belived to be hiding in Serbia since his 1995 indictment on charges of genocide, war crimes and crimes against humanity; US and European Union have already levied sanctions against Serbia for failing to cooperate with United Nations-led tribunal; Pres Boris Tadic, when elected in June, had pledged 'full cooperation' with court (M)

2004-10-01.title: The new chair, made for Japan
2 Car Bombings in Iraq Kill 41, Many Children
2004-10-01.abstract: Two car bombs tear through street celebration in Baghdad, killing 41 Iraqis, at least 34 of them children, and wounding and maiming 139 more; bombers drive their cars into crowds of children waiting to receive candy from American soldiers during festivities marking opening of new sewer plant in capital; attack bespeaks murderous calculation employed by insurgents, who are trying to topple American-backed enterprise in Iraq and who see completion of sewer plant as symbol of progress for that enterprise; 10 American soldiers are wounded in attack; newly refurbished sewage plant escapes unscathed, except for walls that are spattered with flesh and blood of the dead; less than mile away, car bomb crashes into Iraqi National Guard post, causing unknown number of casualties; earlier, another car bomb strikes military checkpoint, killing one American soldier and two Iraqi soldiers and wounding 60 more; Iraq's deputy prime minister Barham Salih blames attacks on terrorists who are trying to derail democratic process in Iraq; photos; map (M)

2004-10-01.title: For travelers from Europe, photographs and fingerprints : Visitors to U.S. get a new welcome

2004-10-02.title: Sudan Agrees to Allow 3,500 African Union Troops Into Darfur
2004-10-02.abstract: Sudan agrees to allow 3,500 African Union troops into Darfur as monitors to oversee Sudanese police; Sudan has been under great pressure to restore security in region; Sudan has previously admitted only 68 unarmed monitors and 308 armed troops to protect them; United Nations special representative Jan Pronk has called for swift increase in number of African Union troops and broadening of their mandate; both Nigeria and Rwanda have committed troops, but logistical problems remain (M)

2004-10-02.title: World Briefing | Europe: Spain: Cabinet Clears Way For Gay Marriage
2004-10-02.abstract: Spanish government approves same-sex marriages and opens door for gays to adopt children; proposal is expected to be approved by Parliament; Spain would become third European nation to legalize gay marriages after Netherlands and Belgium (S)

2004-10-02.title: Aided by Iraqis, U.S. Seizes Part of Rebel Town
2004-10-02.abstract: American and Iraqi troops battle way into heart of Samarra, claiming to have taken more than half of town and killed more than 100 guerrillas; assault represents first major effort to reclaim areas lost to insurgents; follows concern that Samarra insurgents are cooperating with those in Falluja; effort marks largest joint operation with Iraqi forces; map; photos (M)

2004-10-02.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Blair 'Fine' After Heart Procedure
2004-10-02.abstract: British Prime Min Tony Blair returns home from Hammersmith Hospital after successful surgery to correct irregular heartbeat that was first diagnosed in Oct 2003; photo (S)

2004-10-02.title: Gentle growth in Europe, but battle lines are drawn over reform

2004-10-03.title: Sharon to Keep Israeli Troops in Gaza Until Attacks Stop
2004-10-03.abstract: Prime Minister Ariel Sharon said today that Israel was strengthening its military presence in the northern Gaza Strip.

2004-10-03.title: Israeli Forces Kill 9 Palestinians in Military Operation in Gaza
2004-10-03.abstract: Israeli Army kills nine Palestinians in Gaza Strip in on-going campaign to suppress firing of short-range Qassam rockets into Israel; Yasir Arafat and cabinet hold emergency session to denounce world's silence; Ministry of Health in Gaza says 56 Palestinians have died since Sept 29, though it is unclear how many were non-combatants (M)

2004-10-03.title: Americans and Iraqis Press Effort to Secure Samarra
2004-10-03.abstract: American and Iraqi forces continue working to secure Samarra; guerrillas have melted away before advance, but such tactics often presage counterattack; Americans estimate 110 insurgents have been killed and soldiers have captured foreign Arab fighters; plan is for Americans to plow through and subdue city and then hand over security to Iraqi forces; Samarra's hostility to American troops has made it no-go zone over past summer; photo (M)

2004-10-03.title: U.S. and Iraqi Forces Complete Takeover of Samarra
2004-10-03.abstract: U.S. forces said they are beginning the second phase of the operation, turning the city over to Iraqi police and military forces.

2004-10-03.title: End of Era Near in Montreal for White-Elephant Airport
2004-10-03.abstract: Montreal-Mirabel International Airport will close on Oct 31; was promoted as airport of future when it was built at cost of $1 billion in late 1960's and was intended to help Montreal develop as global cultural and financial center; airport was planned to handle 50 million passengers per year, but never had more than 2.8 million; airport will continue to handle cargo flights, but terminal will be converted to other use; photos (M)

2004-10-04.title: ElBaradei, in Seoul, calls for nuclear transparency
Israeli Role in Gaza Strip Is to Expand
2004-10-04.abstract: Prime Min Ariel Sharon says Israel is strengthening its military presence in northern Gaza Strip and will keep troops there until Palestinians stop firing rockets into Israel; in five days of often intense clashes in and around Jabaliya refugee camp, about 60 Palestinian have been killed, including militants and civilians; on Israeli side, two soldiers and three civilians have been killed; photo (M)

2004-10-04.title: Ex-President Says He'll Back Afghan Leader
2004-10-04.abstract: Former Pres Burhanuddin Rabbani, who leads one of largest political parties in Afghanistan, declares his support for Pres Hamid Karzai in presidential election; announcement will further strengthen Karzai, but might also disappoint voters who hoped that new mandate would free him of need to make deals with powerful mujahedeen leaders who dominated politics in country for past two decades (M)

2004-10-04.title: Alliance chief says 3,000 troops may be more than enough : NATO role in training Iraqi Army takes shape

2004-10-05.title: Israeli Missile Strike Kills 2 Senior Figures in Islamic Jihad
2004-10-05.abstract: An Israeli helicopter strike killed two senior members of the radical group Islamic Jihad as they rode in a car in Gaza, Palestinians there said this evening.

2004-10-05.title: Inspector's Report to Detail Iraqi Plans to Undermine Sanctions and Produce Illicit Arms
2004-10-05.abstract: Bush administration officials say report to be made public on Oct 6 by top American weapons inspector Charles A Duelfer will outline new details of attempts by Saddam Hussein's government to undermine United Nations sanctions as part of plan to produce illicit weapons if those sanctions were lifted; say report will make clear that Iraq did not possess stockpiles of illicit weapons at time of American invasion in 2003, and had not begun any large-scale program for weapons production; these findings have previously been reported, but administration officials say final version of document describes new evidence of concerted Iraqi efforts to bypass sanctions while they were still in place and to undermine international support for them (M)

2004-10-05.title: Names of the Dead
2004-10-05.abstract: Defense Dept confirms deaths of six service members in Iraq (S)

2004-10-05.title: World Briefing | Americas: Brazil: Runoff For São Paulo
2004-10-05.abstract: Brazil's governing Workers Party wins mayoral elections outright in 6 of Brazil's 26 states and will compete with opposition Brazilian Social Democratic Party candidates in runoff elections in several cities including Sao Paulo (S)

2004-10-05.title: Lawmakers in Cambodia Vote to Try Khmer Rouge
2004-10-05.abstract: Cambodian Parliament's lower house finally ratifies agreement with United Nations to create international tribunal to try surviving leaders of Communist Khmer Rouge for atrocities during their four-year rule in late 1970's; no timetable is set for trial and some technical and political issues lie ahead, including need to raise estimated $57 million from international donors; none of aging Khmer Rouge leaders have been brought into court; during their reign, as many as one-fourth of Cambodian population died from execution, starvation, overwork and disease; seven major figures are main focus of possible proceedings, but only two are in custody; Khmer Rouge leader Pol Pot died in 1998 (M)

2004-10-06.title: Japan's Troops Proceed in Iraq Without Shot Fired
2004-10-06.abstract: Whenever a Japanese convoy leaves its base here in southern Iraq, the armored vehicles are so spotless that they appear to have just rolled out of a Tokyo car showroom into this crumbling Shiite Muslim town on the Euphrates.     They lack the dents and dirt of other nations' vehicles. Perhaps that is because of the Japanese troops' attention to maintenance or perhaps, as the Iraqis here say, their increasing tendency to stay inside their base with the violence rising outside.

2004-10-06.title: Director of U.N. Nuclear Agency, in Seoul, Cautions Both Koreas
2004-10-06.abstract: Mohamed El Baradei, director of International Atomic Energy Agency, visits Korean peninsula and warns both North and South Korea on nuclear weapons; urges South Korea to maintain control over its program; says he will visit North Korea and urges UN Security Council to have 'smart sanctions' ready to target recalcitrant countries without adding misery to citizens (M)

2004-10-06.title: A New C.I.A. Report Casts Doubt on a Key Terrorist's Tie to Iraq
2004-10-06.abstract: A reassessment by the Central Intelligence Agency has cast doubt on a central piece of evidence used by the Bush administration before the invasion of Iraq to draw links between Saddam Hussein's government and Al Qaeda's terrorist network, government officials said Tuesday.    The C.I.A. report, sent to policy makers in August, says it is now not clear whether Mr. Hussein's government harbored members of a group led by the Jordanian terrorist Abu Musab al-Zarqawi, the officials said. The assertion that Iraq provided refuge to Mr. Zarqawi was the primary basis for the administration's prewar assertions connecting Iraq to Al Qaeda.

2004-10-06.title: World Briefing | Africa: Sudan: No Progress In Darfur Crisis, U.N. Told
2004-10-06.abstract: Jan Pronk, the United Nations envoy to Sudan, told the Security Council that the government in Khartoum has made no progress on protecting the embattled people of the country's Darfur region or in bringing to justice the leaders of rampaging Arab militias. Among the continuing attacks, he said, were those by army helicopters. He said the priority now was in deploying the promised 4,000-man force from the African Union. John C. Danforth, the United States ambassador to the United Nations, said he was concerned about predictions that the full force might not be in place until the end of the year.   Warren Hoge (NYT)

2004-10-06.title: Tory Leader Outlines Domestic Focus for Campaign Against Blair
2004-10-06.abstract: Michael Howard, the leader of Britain's Conservative Party, urged Tories on Tuesday to focus on discontent with Prime Minister Tony Blair's domestic record and his failure to ''tell the truth'' in the months before the war in Iraq as the best strategy for contesting the national elections expected next spring.    In his first address to the annual Conservative Party gathering, Mr. Howard also sought to stem defections to the upstart U.K. Independence Party, which has eroded Conservatives' base by taking a hard line against immigration and membership in the European Union.

2004-10-07.title: Appeal hearings openfor 2 former Elf chiefs
A Step Closer to Europe, Proud Turks Hold Off Glee
2004-10-07.abstract: Turks react with relief to European Commission's qualified endorsement of their country's bid to start talks for membership to European Union; civic and business leaders acknowledge they face more formidable battle to win hearts and minds of European public; many say they would reserve their celebrations for mid-Dec meeting of EU leaders, who will make decision whether to put Turkey on road to eventual entry (M)

2004-10-07.title: World Briefing | Europe: Germany: 5 Go On Trial In Plot On Jewish Center
2004-10-07.abstract: Five neo-Nazis suspected of planning bomb attack on Munich's new Jewish cultural center go on trial; foiled plot, planned for Nov 9, 2003, would have been during cornerstone laying ceremony for center (S)

2004-10-07.title: Report further erodes case on Iraqi weapons
World Briefing | Europe: Russia: 10 Qaeda Agents Said To Be In Caucasus
2004-10-07.abstract: Russian intelligence official Aleksandr Potapov, who heads Chechen operations, reports that more than 10 Qaeda members are in Caucasus region working with insurgents; statement seems to support Russian government hypothesis that Al Qaeda is now collaborating with Chechen militants (S)

2004-10-08.title: The Collections / Paris : Lang ties together the season's strands
Expansion brings EU to foreign policy void
As locusts hit Niger delta, Mali fears famine
Abdication by Cambodia's King Throws His Nation Into Confusion
2004-10-08.abstract: King Norodom Sihanouk's decision to give up throne he has held, off and on, for more than half century throws Cambodia into confusion and doubt; although his power is almost entirely symbolic, he has been soul of Cambodia since he was installed by its French colonial rulers in 1941; he is 81 and in poor health; in recent months he has said he feels marginalized as Cambodian politicians struggle to form government after inconclusive election in July 2003 (M)

2004-10-08.title: The Collections / Paris : Passing the baton at Celine

2004-10-09.title: Peace Prize Goes to Environmentalist in Kenya
2004-10-09.abstract: Nobel Peace Prize is awarded to Dr Wangari Maathai, Kenyan woman who started environmental movement that has planted 30 million trees in Africa; Maathai founded Green Belt Movement in 1977 to organize poor women to fight deforestation and to replenish fuel source; award stretches traditional bounds of prize to include environmental advocacy; Nobel committee calls her 'inspiration for everyone in Africa fighting for sustainable development, democracy and peace'; photo (M)

2004-10-09.title: Names of the Dead
2004-10-09.abstract: Defense Dept confirms death of one American soldier in Iraq, bringing total to 1,063

2004-10-09.title: A Briton in Iraq is beheaded, family says
The Collections / Paris : Lacroix's Mediterranean sun; Chalayan's cruel sea
Inmates Were Reportedly Kept Off Books to Speed Transfer
2004-10-09.abstract: Lt Col Steven L Jordan testified to Army investigators in February that Central Intelligence Agency kept prisoners off books in Iraq to speed their transfer to sites outside Iraq where they would not be protected by Geneva Conventions; says CIA had unwritten agreement with Col Thomas M Pappas, top military intelligence officer at Abu Ghraib prison; others have said that prisoners were kept off books to hide them from Red Cross (M)

2004-10-10.title: Conservatives in Iran Battle the Spread of Foreign Investment
2004-10-10.abstract: Hardline politicians in Iranian Parliament have forced government to cancel contracts with two Turkish companies, and other companies have been scared off; goal of hardliners is to paralyze government and shut it out of international affairs; Ayatollah Ali Khamenei allowed sale of state-owned banks, but Parliament has blocked sale of $5.95 billion in state assets; Pres Mohammad Khatami's moderate government attracted more foreign investment in two years than Iran has had in previous 20 years; photo (M)

2004-10-10.title: Australians Re-electHoward as Prime Minister
2004-10-10.abstract: Australian Prime Min John Howard of Liberal Party is decisively elected to fourth term; preliminary results show Liberals winning 71 of 150 seats in House of Representatives and coalition partner National Party taking 12 seats; Australia's participation in Iraq war is widely unpopular, but booming economy was key issue (M)

2004-10-10.title: Afghan Poll Is Mostly Calm, but Challengers Cry Foul
2004-10-10.abstract: Afghans turn out to vote in large numbers in their first presidential election; unexpectedly peaceful event is marred by 15 candidates' claim of widespread cheating and fraud and request for new vote; criticism casts shadow on what is otherwise historic success for Afghans who have endured more than two decades of war and turmoil; officials say results could take two to three weeks to be tallied; expected threat to elections from Taliban insurgents generally fails to materialize; throughout country, Afghans line up patiently tocast their votes; Pres Bush, campaigning for his re-election, hails arrival of elections in Afghanistan; problem is encountered with ink placed on each voter's thumb to prevent multiple votes; many voters find they could erase it minutes after voting and, if they had extra card, vote again; UN and Afghan officials briefly consider suspending voting, then decide against it; Pres Hamid Karzai's 15 rivals seize on problem and proclaim election illegitimate; photo; diagrams (L)

2004-10-10.title: Scouring Iraq for Enemies, Finding Farmers and Mud
2004-10-10.abstract: Poor intelligence offsets superior American military technology in hunt for Iraqi insurgents; officers maintain optimism, but troops are frustrated; raids may turn up weapons but rarely people; pursuit of fleeing suspects is awkward amid irrigated fields and language difficulties hamper communications with frightened farmers; map; photos (M)

2004-10-10.title: Iraqi Cleric's Militia in Sadr City Promises to Hand Over Arms
2004-10-10.abstract: Iraqi and American officials and aides to rebel Shiite cleric Moktada al-Sadr announce agreement to disband Sadr's militia; say his Mahdi Army fighters will hand over their heavy weapons to Iraqi police next week from their stronghold in Sadr City, following weeks of fighting with American forces; Sadr has been greatly weakened since being upstaged by Grand Ayatollah Ali al-Sisatani; Iraqi and American officials are cautiously optimistic, noting that Sadr has history of renegging on pledges to disarm; his fighters are intensely loyal to him, but some leaders inside movement doubt they would actually obey order to give up their large weapons; policing agreement would be extremely difficult; under accord, American commanders say they would continue to patrol Sadr City, but would not attack Mahdi Army, except in retaliation; any deal that neutralizes Mahdi Army would allow American military and Iraqi government to turn energies toward insurgency's core, in Sunni Arab lands north and west of Baghdad; photo (M)

2004-10-11.title: American and Norwegian Share Economics Nobel
2004-10-11.abstract: Finn E. Kydland and Edward C. Prescott won the 2004 Nobel prize in economics today for their work in determining the driving force behind business cycles worldwide.

2004-10-11.title: 2 Bombs Kill at Least 11 in Baghdad
2004-10-11.abstract: Two suicide car bombs explode within 15 minutes of each other in eastern Baghdad, killing at least 10 Iraqi and American soldiers and wounding 15 others; in northeastern Baghdad, first day of declared cease-fire between American troops and militia of Moktada al-Sadr passes with Sadr's aides saying he is seeking assurances from interim Iraqi government that neither Iraqi security forces nor American troops would arrest or prosecute people in his organization; photos (M)

2004-10-11.title: European monitoring group to observe U.S. election
Car bombs kill 11 in Iraq as Rumsfeld warns of growing violence
U.S. to Push Nations on Economic Aid for Iraq
2004-10-11.abstract: The Bush administration will prod other nations to deepen and speed their economic aid to the embattled country at a conference of donors this week in Tokyo.

2004-10-12.title: U.S. in Talks With Europeans on a Nuclear Deal With Iran
2004-10-12.abstract: Bush administration is talking with European allies about possible package of economic incentives for Iran, including access to imported nuclear fuel, in return for suspension of uranium enrichment activities that are suspected to be part of nuclear arms progrm; diplomats say while administration has not endorsed any incentives for Iran, it is not discouraging Britain, France and Germany from assembling package that administration could consider after American presidential elections on Nov 2 for presentation to Tehran later in month; any support of package of incentives would indicate significant shift in Bush administration policy of demanding penalties, but offering no inducements, to get Iran to halt activities suspected to be for nuclear arms program (M)

2004-10-12.title: The sari saga:Traditional attire loses out to changing styles
Israeli Parliament Votes Its Displeasure With Sharon's Plan for a Gaza Pullout
2004-10-12.abstract: Prime Min Ariel Sharon's plan to withdraw Jewish settlers from Gaza Strip suffers symbolic setback when Parliament votes to reject speech in which he raises proposal; vote is nonbinding, but it demonstrates tough opposition he is facing; Sharon might fare better when actual vote on Gaza proposal is held in Parliament on Oct 25; if he does not win passage of Gaza initiative, it would bode ill for survival of his coalition government; photo (M)

2004-10-12.title: Politicus : French suppress rage over EU constitution
Eggs and Charges Fly in Ukraine's Ever-Nastier Election
2004-10-12.abstract: Pres Leonid D Kuchma's decision not to seek third five-year term opens fierce and increasingly nasty presidentical campaign in Ukraine; polls suggest fierce race between two most prominent candidates: former prime minister Viktor A Yushchenko, leader of anti-Kuchma opposition, and current prime minister Viktor Yanukovich, who promises to carry out Kuchma's legacy; neither front-runner is expected to win outright majority in elections Oct 31; runoff is scheduled for Nov 21; photos (M)

2004-10-13.title: 9 Die in Japan Suicides Tied to Web
2004-10-13.abstract: Nine people, who apparently met and planned suicide over internet, are found dead in two rental cars; bodies were in sealed vehicles with charcoal burners at their feet; seven people, including some teenagers, were found parked on mountain road in Tokyo suburb; other rental car was found 75 miles south and contained two women; Japan had record 34,427 cases of suicide in 2003; National Police Agency reports that 45 people committed suicide in groups from Jan 2003 to June 2004 in Japan (M)

2004-10-13.title: A dispute over man who fought for Hitler : Spain reopens old wound
Germany Rejects Speculation That Iraq Policy May Change
2004-10-13.abstract: German officials today reaffirmed their policy of not contributing troops to the American-led coalition in Iraq.

2004-10-13.title: Globalist : The Serbian question, still on Europe's plate

2004-10-14.title: World Briefing | Asia: Indonesia: Mining Employees Stay In Custody
2004-10-14.abstract: Lawyer Luthfi Yazid reports that Indonesian police have extended detention of his clients, five Newmont Mining Corp employees, for another 40 days; authorities allege that waste from Minahasa mine has polluted water supply (S)

2004-10-14.title: Charting grisly evidence in Iraqi desert
World Briefing | Europe: Turkey: Extradited Militant Held On Treason
2004-10-14.abstract: Militant Muslim cleric Metin Kaplan, who was just extradited to Turkey from Germany, is charged with treason; is accused of plotting to crash explosive-laden airplane into Ataturk mausoleum and planning to overthrow secular Turkish government (S)

2004-10-14.title: Iraq Demands Falluja Give Up Its Militants From Abroad
2004-10-14.abstract: Prime Min Ayad Allawi warns insurgents in Falluja that they must hand over foreign militants or face military operation; ultimatum comes as group led by foreign militant Abu Musab al-Zarqawi of Jordan posts another video showing two Iraqi men being beheaded; six American soldiers are reported killed in insurgent attacks in previous 24 hours, two in suicide blasts and four in roadside bombings; photo (M)

2004-10-15.title: Taiwan denies that it tested plutonium
U.S. Marines push ahead with air, ground assaults against Fallujah
Malaria Vaccine Proves Effective
2004-10-15.abstract: Researchers say vaccine against malaria has shown for first time that it can save children from infection or death; vaccine, tested on thousands of children in Mozambique, is hardly perfect: it protected them from catching disease only about 30 percent of time and prevented it from becoming life-threatening only about 58 percent of time; but because malaria kills more than million people a year, 700,000 of them children, even partial protection would be public health victory; disease, caused by parasite carried by mosquitoes, is found in 90 countries, and drug-resistant strains are spreading; director of Malaria Vaccine Initiative, which is underwriting tests on 15 experimental vaccines with money from Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, says that GlaxoSmithKline product tried in Mozambique is now its leading candidate and had proved that concept works; photo of mosquito (L)

2004-10-15.title: Afghan vote counting begins, but with delays

2004-10-16.title: You don't eat meat? Have some prosciutto!
From Afghanistan to Saudi Arabia, via Guantánamo
2004-10-16.abstract: YASER E. HAMDI, a high school honor student from a privileged Saudi background, was a sophomore in college, majoring in marketing, when he took off for Afghanistan during the summer of 2001.     His father, who spells his name Himdy, says it was just one of those things young men do, to find themselves or to satisfy a yearning for adventure. ''Boys like to try things new,'' Mr. Himdy said. ''He had never traveled outside Saudi Arabia before. He decided to show, 'I am a man.'''

2004-10-16.title: Vowing Iraq pullback, Polish leader wins vote
Names of the Dead
2004-10-16.abstract: The Department of Defense has identified 1,082 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war. It confirmed the deaths of the following Americans yesterday:    BAKER, Ronald W., 34, Specialist, Army National Guard; Cabot, Ark.; 39th Support Battalion.    REGNIER, Jeremy F., 22, Specialist, Army; Littleton, N.H.; First Cavalry Division.

2004-10-16.title: U.S. Intensifying Bombing Attacks on Falluja Sites
2004-10-16.abstract: American forces stepped up their pressure on the insurgent-controlled city of Falluja on Friday, unleashing an aerial and artillery bombardment of suspected guerrilla positions and encircling the city with hundreds of marines at checkpoints along its main roads.    Beginning Thursday, American jets began their most intense bombardment of the city yet, dropping 11 satellite- and laser-guided 500-pound bombs on suspected guerrilla safe houses and weapons depots, as 1,600 American marines moved forward under artillery fire to set up the checkpoints, designed to snare insurgents moving out of the city.

2004-10-17.title: 6 Killed in Attacks In 2 Afghan Regions
2004-10-17.abstract: Three children, two American soldiers and an Afghan policeman died in two new attacks by people suspected of being Taliban rebels in Afghanistan, Afghan and American officials announced Saturday.     Two American soldiers died and three others were wounded on Thursday when an improvised bomb detonated under their vehicle in Uruzgan Province in southern Afghanistan, the American military officials said.

2004-10-17.title: Iraq Commanders Warn That Delays in Civil Projects Undermine Military Mission
2004-10-17.abstract: American military commanders in Iraq are warning civilian leaders in Washington that delays in reconstruction projects caused by red tape are putting the lives of their troops at risk and undermining the military mission in Iraq.     From the junior officer level up through the senior ranks, these officers have outlined obstacles that they say frustrate the rebuilding just as much as the roadside bombs and terrorist attacks that are the more visible threats. They have argued to the new American Embassy in Baghdad and to Congressional delegations visiting Iraq that certain regulations are a dangerous impediment to the military's mission.

2004-10-17.title: Dispute Raises Questions About Democracy in the European Union
Free of Taliban's Yoke, 2 Afghan Women Rise Again
2004-10-17.abstract: The first sign of change is a sign, posted on the brown mud exterior wall of Soheila Helal's house and garden to announce her private courses. When the Taliban controlled this western city, Ms. Helal had to teach in secret. Now she is free to advertise.     Nearly three years ago, days after the Taliban left the city ending almost six years of repressive rule, Ms. Helal was one of a host of women interviewed by The New York Times. They recounted lives cloistered and hopes curtailed through days that blurred to months, then years.

2004-10-17.title: Sharon Rejects Voteon Jewish Settlers
2004-10-17.abstract: The Israeli prime minister flatly rejected today a demand by Jewish settler leaders that he hold a national referendum.

2004-10-18.title: In storm-ravaged Haiti, political tension rises
News Analysis: Questions arise on democracy at the EU
Israel Feuds With Agency Set Up to Aid Palestinians
2004-10-18.abstract: Israel and United Nations Relief and Works Agency have had running battle; Israel claims agency's Palestinian workers help Palestinian militants, and that militants have often gained access to UN ambulances, schools and other facilities; agency's director Peter Hansen says Israel has frequently raised false or inflated accusations against it and has consistently hampered aid efforts by imposing stringent travel restrictions against its personnel in West Bank and Gaza Strip; since 2001, Israel has arrested and indicted 17 of agency's workers, accusing them of assisting militants; seven have been convicted by military courts; in incident in Gaza City in May, four Palestinian gunmen climbed into UN ambulance after placing wounded comrade inside; incident was captured by television cameraman, and Israeli military contends Palestinians used ambulance to escape; photo (M)

2004-10-18.title: Europe Struggling to Train New Breed of Muslim Clerics
2004-10-18.abstract: Effort is under way in Europe to train clerics to minister to growing population of Muslims in Europe, in hope of stanching migration of clerics who are beholden to foreign interests and, in some cases, full of hate and capable of terrorist acts; perceived threat is so great that some European governments closely monitor activities and sermons of their Muslim clerics; France has expelled more than dozen Muslim clerics for violations of human rights or public order since 2001; in Italy, Senegalese-born imam was expelled after calling for suicide bombings and declaring 'blood pact' with Osama bin Laden; Britian is charging militant Muslim cleric Abu Hamza al-Masri, supporter of Osama bin Laden, with terrorism offenses, stalling American effort to extradite him to United States; creating army of learned, law-abiding, Europeanized imams will not be easy because in Europe, government involvement in religion can be seen as violation of privacy and human rights; photos; map (M)

2004-10-19.title: World Briefing | Americas: Canada: Call To Stop Export Of Drugs
2004-10-19.abstract: A coalition of health care groups is warning that the sale of cheaper prescription drugs to customers in the United States could lead to drug shortages in Canada. The group, Best Medicines Coalition, is urging the federal government to stop the growing Internet trade in drugs, which are cheaper in Canada because of government price controls. While importing drugs from Canada has been offered as a solution to their high cost in the United States, most notably by Senator John Kerry, many health care experts in Canada say the country cannot support a huge increase in demand. ''They are talking about raiding our medicine cabinet,'' said Louise Binder, a member of the coalition.   Colin Campbell (NYT)

2004-10-19.title: Spitting incident fuels debate on intolerance
Jeb Bush rules out 2008 presidential run
Relief Agency Official Is Kidnapped in Baghdad
2004-10-19.abstract: Margaret Hassan, who married an Iraqi and has lived in Iraq for more than 30 years, has been involved in aid work for 25 years.

2004-10-19.title: World Briefing | Americas: Haiti: South Africa Denies Aristide Claims
2004-10-19.abstract: South Africa's Foreign Ministry rejected ''with contempt'' an accusation by Haiti's prime minister, Gérard Latortue, that President Thabo Mbeki had allowed the deposed Haitian president, Jean-Bertrand Aristide, now in exile in South Africa at the Mbeki government's expense, to mount a campaign to destabilize his old homeland. At least 50 people have died in recent violence in Haiti fomented largely by pro-Aristide gangs. Mr. Latortue was quoted Sunday as saying that Mr. Mbeki had ignored international law by giving Mr. Aristide free rein ''to organize violence'' in Haiti from his South African refuge.   Michael Wines (NYT)

2004-10-20.title: Foes of Death Penalty Making Gradual Gains in Africa
2004-10-20.abstract: Edmary Mpagi, who spent 18 years on Uganda's death row before being acquitted of crime, is part of growing movement to eliminate death penalty in Africa; 15 years ago, only Cape Verde Islands did not have death penalty; now 10 countries have outlawed practice and 10 more have abolished its use; proponents of death penalty include religious leaders, particularly Islamic courts, and citizens who fear violent criminal activity; Uganda, which has 417 people on death row, supports harsh penalties, but has not carried out execution since March 2003; Uganda Citizen's Rescue activist Godfrey Ssebuwufu supports abolishing capital punishment; investigates cases of those individuals on death row and seeks to have verdicts overturned; Edmary Mpagi was released as result of Ssebuwufu's diligence to save innocent lives; photo (M)

2004-10-20.title: World Briefing | United Nations: Renewed Call For Syria To Leave Lebanon
2004-10-20.abstract: United Nations Security Council urges Syria to comply with Sept 2 resolution that all foreign troops pull out of Lebanon; calls for Syria to withdraw 14,000 troops believed to be in place there; Syrian Foreign Minister Farouk al-Sharaa and Lebanese Foreign Ministry hold United Nations has no jurisdiction in bilateral relations between nations (S)

2004-10-20.title: World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: 246 Militants Killed Since Spring
2004-10-20.abstract: Pakistani Lt Gen Safdar Hussain Shah reports that 171 Pakistani soldiers and paramilitary forces have died since start of spring offensive along Pakistan-Afghanistan border; estimates that just 100 militants remain in region and does not believe Osama bin Laden could be hiding there; 500 to 600 militants were believed to have been in region prior to intensified efforts (S)

2004-10-20.title: Myanmar junta replacesprime minister; house arrest reported

2004-10-21.title: Sunnis threaten election boycott over Falluja siege
U.S. ballots being posted on Web site
18 Die as Season's 10th Typhoon Rakes Japan, Breaking Record
2004-10-21.abstract: Powerful typhoon roars through Japan's main islands, killing at least 30 people and injuring more than 250 in mudslides and flash floods; 40 people are missing; power failures affect one million people; this is 10th typhoon to strike Japan's main islands this year, breaking record of six (M)

2004-10-21.title: CAMPAIGN 2004: New option for absentee U.S. voters
Britain to Send 850 Troops Toward Baghdad
2004-10-21.abstract: The troops will replace U.S. fighting units that are expected to mount an assault on Iraqi insurgents west of the capital near Falluja.

2004-10-22.title: Israeli Missile Kills Hamas Weapons Maker
2004-10-22.abstract: Israeli airstrike in Gaza City kills Adnan al-Ghoul, leading weapons maker of Hamas who was responsible for some of group's most powerful bombs and homemade rockets; this is latest in series of Israeli strikes against most senior Hamas figures (S)

2004-10-22.title: Names of the Dead
2004-10-22.abstract: Defense Dept confirms death of one service member in Iraq, bringing total to 1,100 dead since start of Iraq war (S)

2004-10-22.title: Outside U.S., supplies of vaccine are normal
Barroso proposals fail to quell revolt
Europeans Offers Plan to End Standoff on Iran Nuclear Program
2004-10-22.abstract: Britain, France and Germany are working to give Iran a last chance to avoid confrontation with the West over its nuclear program by offering incentives to curtail its most sensitive activities.

2004-10-23.title: Expat voters face range of snags
Blood donor has mad cow disease
Chechen Rebel Leader Is Reported to Be Close to Surrendering
2004-10-23.abstract: The senior law enforcement official in Chechnya announced Friday that the authorities had nearly captured Aslan Maskhadov, one of the best-known leaders of the Chechen resistance, and that Mr. Maskhadov was planning to surrender soon.     The announcement, made by the first deputy prime minister, Ramzan Kadyrov, in Grozny, the Chechen capital, underscored the sense of urgency driving the hunts for senior separatists. It also exposed the tension among the Russian security agencies conducting them.

2004-10-23.title: Still Deeply Divided, Nervous Kosovo Goes to Polls This Weekend
2004-10-23.abstract: Five years after the United Nations took over the administration of Kosovo, the southern province of Serbia, the residents vote this weekend in an election that may be crucial in determining whether it becomes independent.    The vote for the 120-seat Parliament is the second since NATO troops forced Serbian security forces responsible for widespread atrocities out of the province in June 1999, making Kosovo an international protectorate. Half a decade later, Kosovo remains home to NATO's largest peacekeeping force, and the election has focused the minds of the ethnic Albanian majority, who make up over 90 percent of the 1.8 million people, most of whom are impatient for an end to the international control.

2004-10-23.title: Kosovo election a crucial test for independence

2004-10-24.title: Exhuming a Political Killing Reopens Old Wounds in Brazil
2004-10-24.abstract: Vladimir Herzog died in military custody nearly 30 years ago in what remains one of the most notorious cases of human rights abuse in Brazil. Now the publication of a pair of photographs said to have been taken in his last hours has reopened that old wound and widened differences between the armed forces and the left-wing government that is now in office here.    The army's attempt, decades after the episode, to justify its treatment of Mr. Herzog and several hundred other political prisoners has enraged public opinion here. Though President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva has moved to discipline the army, the resurfacing of the case has also exposed other violations that may prove harder to address.

2004-10-24.title: Rebel Attacks Kill 18 Iraqis; G.I.'s Injured
2004-10-24.abstract: Insurgents mount strikes on United States and Iraqi outposts across Iraq, using mortars and hidden roadside bombs to kill at least 18 Iraqi police officers and national guardsmen and wound dozens of Iraqis and Americans; American military official says attacks do not appear to be coordinated; Army of Ansar al-Sunna, militant Iraqi group that has taken credit for string of gruesome killings, posts video on Internet showing beheading of man they call 'crusader spy' for American forces; news agencies report that man claimed he sold drinks to American base in Mosul; flurry of violence underscores challenge facing American and Iraqi officials as planned national elections draw near; photo; map (M)

2004-10-24.title: Dozens of Iraqi soldiers found shot to death
Dutch Soldiers Find Smiles Are a More Effective Protection
2004-10-24.abstract: In a neighborhood here without lights, its pockmarked dirt streets and open sewers faintly visible under the full moon, the Dutch soldiers began a foot patrol on a recent evening. After getting out of their soft-top vehicles, the soldiers entered a street, wearing no helmets and pointing their guns down, chatting with Iraqis clustered in front of their homes.    ''Hello, Mister!'' some boys cried out, and they followed the soldiers to the bend in the road. Driving through the town later, the Dutch called out ''Salaam Aleikum'' to pedestrians. Many Iraqis, adults and children, waved at them.

2004-10-24.title: Turkey's Law Overhaul Overwhelms Courts and Citizens
2004-10-24.abstract: In Judge Dursun Genel's snug little courtroom, the feuding couples shuttle in and out with stories of exhausted dreams and unhappy marriages.    ''We'll never have peace,'' a young woman recently told the judge, agreeing with her estranged husband that the only solution to their problems was divorce.

2004-10-25.title: American Killed in Bombing Was Due Home Soon
2004-10-25.abstract: Stepfather of Jamie Michalsky, 23-year-old American woman killed in suicide bomb attack in Kabul on Oct 23, says she was due to return from Afghanistan in December; she was working as linguist with WorldWide Language Resources Inc; photo of bomb scene (M)

2004-10-25.title: Iran Rejects Nuclear Plan as Imbalanced, Europe Is Told
2004-10-25.abstract: Iran rejects proposal by Britain, Germany and France to suspend its uranium enrichment program in return for guarantee to help Iran build light-water power reactor and to provide supply of reactor fuel; urges those countries to offer 'more balanced' proposal (M)

2004-10-25.title: Top Army Official Calls fora Halliburton Inquiry
2004-10-25.abstract: Bunnatine H Greenhouse, chief contracting officer of Army Corps of Engineers, charges that Army gave Halliburton Co large contracts for work in Iraq and Balkans without following rules designed to ensure competition and fair prices to government; calls for high-level investigation of what she describes as threat to integrity of federal contracting program; says that after her repeated questions about Halliburton contracts, she was excluded from major decisions to award money and that her job status was threatened; Army refers Greenhouse's accusations to Pentagon's investigations bureau for review and promises to protect her position in meantime; photo (M)

2004-10-25.title: New Guerrilla Factions Arise in Sudan Ahead of Peace Talks
2004-10-25.abstract: Two new guerrilla factions emerge in western Sudan, potentially complicating peace talks that are set to begin between Sudanese government and its two established rebel foes; one is based near northwestern border with Chad and other in southern Darfur; rebel and government delegates to peace talks accuse one another of bad faith (M)

2004-10-26.title: U.S. Action Bars Right of Some Captured in Iraq
2004-10-26.abstract: Bush administration says it has concluded that some non-Iraqi prisoners captured by American forces in Iraq are not entitled to Geneva Conventions protections; this is important exception to public assertions by administration since Mar 2003; new opinion would essentially allow military and CIA to treat at least small number of non-Iraqi prisoners captured in Iraq in same way as members of Al Qaeda and Taliban captured in Afghanistan and elsewhere, for whom US has maintained that Geneva Conventions do not apply; at issue are CIA's secret transfers of dozen non-Iraqi prisoners for interrogation outside Iraq, and provision in conventions that bars such transfers; administration officials say new opinion represents consensus reached by lawyers from State Dept, Justice Dept, Pentagon, National Security Council and other agencies in discussions that began in Mar 2004 in response to opinion sought by CIA many months after it began transferring non-Iraqi prisoners beginning in Apr 2003 (M)

2004-10-26.title: Inquiry Into Ambush Opens; Iraqi Forces Feared Infiltrated
2004-10-26.abstract: Iraqi officials open investigation into role played by infiltrators in ambush that left 49 Iraqi National Guard trainees dead; there are growing indications that insurgents are being given inside information about movements of Iraqi security forces; United States military officials have long been skeptical of loyalty of Iraqi security forces, having seen some American-trained Iraqi soldiers take up arms against occupation forces during fighting in Apr; now even senior Iraqi government officials concede possibility that insurgents staged attack with help from members of Iraqi security forces; photo (M)

2004-10-26.title: World Briefing | Africa: South Africa: Zimbabwean Opposition Visits
2004-10-26.abstract: The leader of Zimbabwe's political opposition, Morgan Tsvangirai, met with President Thabo Mbeki in Pretoria. It was Mr. Tsvangirai's first foreign trip since Zimbabwe officials seized his passport and charged him with treason 32 months ago; he was acquitted on Oct. 15. Mr. Mbeki has been working to open a dialogue between the opposition and President Robert Mugabe.   Michael Wines (NYT)

2004-10-26.title: 4 years after Milosevic, unity's gone in Belgrade
Former military budget chief faces court-martial in scandal : Arroyo takes aim at graft in army

2004-10-27.title: Pakistan Tries to Curb 'Honor Killings'
2004-10-27.abstract: Pakistan's lower house passes government-backed legislation to introduce death penalty for 'honor killings' that claim lives of hundreds of women each year (S)

2004-10-27.title: Planning an estate to preserve a family
Fingerprinting Glitches Are Said to Hurt Antiterror Effort
2004-10-27.abstract: Problems in searching fingerprint databases have left US military unable to check fully identities of thousands of detainees in Iraq and Afghanistan, raising concerns about premature releases of terrorist suspects; Defense Department biometrics officer discusses efforts to address FBI complaints that Pentagon data are not compatible with FBI's use of customized portable units, which meet global standards; photo (M)

2004-10-27.title: As property dulls, cash and shares look brighter
Polls show passions about election reflect wide dislike of Bush : Vote in U.S. inflames Europeans

2004-10-28.title: Rescue of Toddler From Rubble 4 Days After Quake Lifts Japan's Spirit
2004-10-28.abstract: Japanese firefighters rescue boy, 2, from van buried by rock slide touched off by earthquake, Nagaoka; rescue is broadcast live, lifting nation's spirits after quake that left 33 dead, over 3,500 hurt and over 100,000 people living in shelters; others spend night in cars or garages, ready to flee new tremors; Japan, long accustomed to generously giving foreign aid, accepts emergency aid from South Korea and United States; boy's mother is pulled from wreakage, dead; his sister is also believed dead; map; photos (M)

2004-10-28.title: Political Power of Turkish Army Seems to Fade
2004-10-28.abstract: Civilian, Yigit Alpogan, presides over Turkey's National Security Council for first time since 1980 military coup, reflecting quiet but major shift toward limiting military's political power; his appointment as secretary general followed constitutional amendments that cut number of posts reserved for military in council and several other government institutions; council has been army's vehicle for communicating its views on variety of issues (M)

2004-10-28.title: At Syrian border, a hope for stability : Minefields loom as Turks await EU
Remark on Homosexuality Delays Seating of European Panel
2004-10-28.abstract: Jose Barroso, set to take over presidency of European Commission, European Union's executive body, withdraws his slate of commissioners rather than withdraw nomination of Rocco Buttiglione as commissioner for justice, freedom and security; Buttiglione raised hackles amoung union's left-leaning legislators at confirmation hearing by calling homosexuality 'sin;' commission of current president, Romano Prodi, will stay on as caretaker until Barroso presents new lineup to Parliament; dispute suggests there is some life in Parliament, often maligned as costly and moribund (M)

2004-10-28.title: Warnings by Powell to Taiwan Provoke a Diplomatic Dispute
2004-10-28.abstract: Chinese officials laud State Sec Colin L Powell for warning Taiwan in television interviews during brief visit to Beijing that it is not independent nation and that United States favors its 'peaceful reunification' with China; Taiwanese leaders score him for changing longstanding policy; US has recognized China's claim that there is only 'one China,' including Taiwan, but has not explicitly backed reunification; State Department denies policy change; Pres Chen Shui-bian insists Taiwan i

2004-10-29.title: F.B.I. Investigating Contracts With Halliburton
2004-10-29.abstract: Federal Bureau of Investigation wants to question Bunnatine H Greenhouse, chief of contracting with Army Corps of Engineers, about letter she sent to acting Army secretary in which she charged that officials showed favoritism toward Halliburton Co in awarding and oversight of oil contracts in Iraq; she also said officials at Army Corps of Engineers tried to remove her as chief contract monitor after she raised persistent questions about Halliburton contracts; it is not clear whether decision to question her reflects expansion of criminal inquiry on fuel charges into broader, more politically explosive allegation of favoritism in initial award (M)

2004-10-29.title: CAMPAIGN 2004 : Kerry presses Bush over Iraq arms
Iraqi Minister Says U.N. Is Lax in Providing Election Help
2004-10-29.abstract: Foreign Min Hoshyar Zebari of Iraq says Untied Nations is still not doing enough to help organize elections scheduled for Jan; UN has team of 35 workers in Iraq, only eight of whom are from its electoral assistance unit; spokesman for Secretary General Kofi Annan says he cannot send more people until security sitation improves; photo (M)

2004-10-29.title: Terror links alleged in a power struggle : Dispute splits Bulgaria's Muslims
World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Siberian Mine Blast Kills 13
2004-10-29.abstract: An explosion in a coal mine in the southern Siberian region of Kemerovo killed 13 people and injured 11, and prompted a criminal investigation into safety precautions at the site, Russian news agencies reported. At least 103 people were in the mine at the time, the authorities said. They described the cause as a buildup of methane, which ignited.   C.J. Chivers (NYT)

2004-10-30.title: Germany asking if queen will apologize
Names of the Dead
2004-10-30.abstract: The Department of Defense has identified 1,106 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war. It confirmed the death of the following American yesterday:    LEMON, Jerome, 42, Staff Sgt., Army National Guard; North Charleston, S.C.; 1052nd Transportation Company.

2004-10-30.title: A Traveler Loses Himself in Middle Eastern Music
2004-10-30.abstract: THE weekly zhikr ceremony organized by a brotherhood of Sufi Muslims noisily escalates toward its climax, with some 50 male participants in a double circle grunting God's name repeatedly at an ever faster pace while swinging rhythmically from side to side.    Julien Jalal Eddine Weiss loses himself in the choreographed prayers, rocking in a place of honor near a renowned Sufi troubadour wearing a large green turban and long white robe, plus several religious grandees wearing beanies and lengthy beards.

2004-10-30.title: Study puts civilian toll in Iraq at over 100,000
Pentagon Extends Tours of Duty for About 6,500 U.S. Soldiers
2004-10-30.abstract: The Pentagon has ordered about 6,500 soldiers in Iraq to extend their tours, the first step the military has taken to increase its combat power there in preparation for the January elections, senior Defense Department officials said Friday.     About 3,500 members of the Second Brigade of the First Cavalry Division will stay in Iraq two months longer than initially ordered, and about 3,000 soldiers assigned to headquarters and support units of the First Infantry Division will have their tours extended by two and a half weeks.

2004-10-31.title: Eerie Scene at Iraqi Arms Site
2004-10-31.abstract: More than a year and a half after hundreds of tons of explosives vanished at Al Qaqaa, the Iraqi weapons facility, the scene is a wasteland, ruled by mujahedeen. Page 12.

2004-10-31.title: Belatedly, Africa Is Converting to Lead-Free Gasoline
2004-10-31.abstract: When Kenyans go to the gas pump, they have several options -- regular, super, premium, diesel and, increasingly, a choice that has many local motorists scratching their heads, unleaded.    Lead-free gasoline may be the fuel of choice in most of the world but sub-Saharan Africa lags years behind when it comes to converting to cleaner-burning gasoline. On this continent, gas is sold at slick, modern stations and at roadside stands, where vendors use their mouths to help siphon fuel from plastic jugs. Many motorists mistakenly regard unleaded gas as suitable only for the latest model cars.

2004-10-31.title: Ethnic Clashes Erupt in China, Leaving 150 Dead
2004-10-31.abstract: Violent clashes between members of the Muslim Hui ethnic group and the majority Han group left nearly 150 people dead, journalists and witnesses in the region said today.

2004-10-31.title: Afghan Militants Repeat Threat to Kill 3
2004-10-31.abstract: A militant group claiming to have kidnapped three foreign election workers in Afghanistan told news agencies on Saturday that the hostages would be killed unless all Taliban prisoners in Afghanistan and in Guantánamo Bay, Cuba, were released.    The group also called for international and Afghan forces hunting for the kidnappers to stop their pursuit, repeating a threat to kill the hostages if the pursuit continued.

2004-10-31.title: Mexico City Mayor Faces Move to Lift His Immunity
2004-10-31.abstract: At 6:30 every morning in recent days, Andrés Manuel López Obrador, the leftist mayor of this giant city and arguably the most popular politician in the country, strides to a podium to face a gang of reporters. They are all bristling with questions about possible corruption in his government and a looming vote in Congress that could knock him off Mexico's presidential ballot in 2006.    A year ago, Mr. López, a populist with a history of organizing street protests to get his way, was considered by many pollsters as the most likely candidate to win the presidency in 2006 and the first candidate from the left-wing Democratic Revolutionary Party with a real chance of taking power.
